nativeRange.habitat: In nature, Mini Pellia grows as an epiphyte and epilith — a plant that lives on the surfaces of rocks, wood, and other substrates rather than rooted in soil — in damp, shaded forests. It is native to southeastern Asia and has naturalized across Europe, North Africa, East Asia, North America, and tropical South America.

decorPreferences: stainless steel or plastic mesh for detail-work patches
attached with cyanoacrylate gel, fishing line, or wedged into hardscape crevices

avoidWith: warm tropical tanks held consistently above ~28 °C

summary: Mini Pellia is a slow-growing liverwort that forms intricate, coral-like dark-green cushions when submerged. It is generally attached to rock or driftwood rather than planted in substrate, and tends to thrive best with CO2 supplementation, low-to-medium light, and stable, clean water.
careNotes: Riccardia chamedryfolia is a delicate liverwort widely traded as 'Mini Pellia' or 'Coral Moss' because its tightly branched, narrow, ascending thalli form compact cushions that resemble miniature stony-coral colonies when submerged. It is taxonomically and visually distinct from Monosolenium tenerum (sold confusingly as 'Pellia', fw-plant-153), which is a flat, broad, ribbon-like thallose liverwort — Mini Pellia is markedly smaller (1–3 cm cushions vs Monosolenium's 1–5 cm flat blades) and has a finer, more branched habit prized for detail aquascaping. ATTACHMENT: this is an epiphyte — fix small portions to porous lava rock, pebbles, twigs, branching driftwood, or stainless/plastic mesh using gel cyanoacrylate, fine fishing line, or by wedging into crevices. It does not naturally cling at first, but once established the rhizoids hold position reliably and the cushion can be left to thicken. Never bury in substrate. LIGHTING is best in the low-to-medium range; intense light tends to encourage algae overgrowth before the slow-growing cushion can establish coverage. CO2 supplementation is generally considered optional at a basic level but recommended by most aquascaping sources, because growth is reportedly minimal without it and achieving the dense, coral-like cushion look requires both CO2 and a regular trace-element fertilization regimen. GROWTH RATE is slow — commonly reported at 1–2 cm per month even under optimal conditions — so patience and pristine, well-filtered water are important; Mini Pellia is sensitive to organic buildup and to drastic parameter swings, either of which can trigger melting. SHRIMP-TANK ROLE: the dense cushions are excellent biofilm habitat for Neocaridina and Caridina dwarf shrimp, and the plant is widely used in detail-oriented shrimp aquascapes.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only in aquarium conditions: split established cushions into smaller portions, or cut off marginal daughter plants, and reattach each fragment to fresh hardscape with cyanoacrylate gel or fine line. Every healthy fragment regrows. Can also be grown emersed on moist substrates, where it resembles its wild forest-floor form; sexual reproduction (spore production) is essentially never observed in submerged aquarium setups.

primary.notes: Primary product page used after the originally-assigned URL (/products/mini-pellia) returned 404. Used for: common names (Mini Pellia, Coral Moss), liverwort classification, pH 6–8, temperature ceiling (≃83 °F / 28 °C; melts in warmer water), low-to-medium lighting, CO2 not necessary but recommended for better growth and coloration, easy care rating, epiphyte placement, attachment to driftwood/stones/hardscape, shrimp/fish shade and coverage role, hardiness across parameters, brown bits being normal in low-tech setups, and parameter-stability warnings to avoid melting.

additional.name: Aquasabi — Riccardia sp. 'chamedryfolia'
additional.notes: Secondary cross-verification (used after the originally-assigned aquasabi.com/aquatic-plant-lexicon/riccardia-chamedryfolia URL returned 404). Used for: family Aneuraceae, temperature 18–28 °C, pH 5–7, gH 0–30, kH 0–14, height 1–3 cm, medium lighting, CO2 recommended, slow growth rate, medium difficulty, epiphyte placement (attaches to wood/rock via rhizoids), suitability for nano aquariums, propagation by splitting and removing daughter plants, and ability to grow emersed. Slight disagreement with Buce on pH (5–7 vs 6–8) and care level (medium vs easy) — averaged to pH 5.5–7.5 and careLevel intermediate per the playbook's disagreement-averaging rule.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plants Database — Riccardia chamedryfolia
additional.notes: Tertiary taxonomic and identity check. Used for: family Aneuraceae, order Metzgeriales, native range (N Africa, Europe, E Asia, N America, tropical S America), epiphyte/epilith form, propagation by splitting and daughter plants, and ability to grow emersed. Flowgrow explicitly notes 'There is no detailed information for cultivating this plant yet' and warns the name is sometimes erroneously applied to related Riccardia taxa in the trade — documented in careNotes.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Riccardia chamedryfolia
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Used for: scientific authority '(With.) Grolle', confirmation of common names (Mini Pellia, Coral Pellia, Jagged Germanderwort), native habitat (damp forests of southeastern Asia), naturalized global spread, and the 'coral-like growth when submerged' description that motivates the Coral Moss trade name.

propagation: glue to rock or wood with cyanoacrylate gel
tie with fine fishing line or thread until rhizoids anchor
